Outside view, country-month ACLED strategic-development counts do spike around elections or acute political/security crises, but >10x jumps over a recent annual baseline are rare. I therefore forecast low probabilities throughout, with later dates modestly closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty and election-related tail risk.

Reference class: ACLED-coded riot events in a very small, wealthy, highly policed microstate. The known baseline just before the forecast date is effectively zero: the most recent published 30-day average of riots over the prior 360 days in Monaco was 0.000 as of 2025-10-16, strongly suggesting no recent riot activity and likely a long-run near-zero riot frequency. For this market, that usually means the practical question is whether ACLED will record at least one riot in Monaco in each target 30-day window. Monaco’s small population, limited contentious politics, and strong state capacity make that uncommon. Reference class: 30-day ACLED protest counts in a country with sustained, high-frequency contention, compared against a fixed trailing 12-month 30-day average. Outside view, a future 30-day block is roughly 50/50 to land above or below a trailing mean. I then adjust for the calendar and persistence. The benchmark is already high (~128 protests per 30 days), so clearing it requires a genuinely strong month, not merely normal activity. For 2025-11-02, the 30-day window (roughly early Oct to early Nov) overlaps much of the October holiday period, which plausibly suppresses routine mobilization, so I put it modestly below even odds. For 2025-11-25, the window is a full post-holiday month in a setting with persistent weekly anti-government/hostage/war-related demonstrations, so I put it modestly above even odds. For 2026-01-24, I pull back toward 0.5 because of longer horizon, possible mean reversion/fatigue, weather, and general uncertainty.

Reference class: 30-day ACLED 'Explosions/Remote violence' counts in an active conflict theater, compared against a fixed trailing-12-month 30-day average. The stated benchmark is about 11.25 as of 2025-10-16, so the practical hurdle is likely around 12 events in the 30 days before resolution. If future counts were roughly Poisson with mean near that benchmark, the chance of clearing 12 would be a bit under 50% (around the high-40s). In real conflict data, counts are usually overdispersed and right-skewed, which tends to make the probability of exceeding the mean slightly lower than the Poisson benchmark. That pushes me modestly below 50% overall. I keep the later dates a bit closer to 0.5 because uncertainty increases with horizon, even though there may be some seasonal/logistical reasons late-year windows would not obviously be above an already-elevated annual average.

Reference class: 30-day protest counts in Martinique relative to that territory’s own trailing 360-day average. The prompt says the most recent known baseline was 3.167 protests per 30 days as of 2025-10-16, which implies a likely decision threshold of about 4 protests in a 30-day window unless the final 2025-10-26 rolling average falls below 3. Under a simple stationary count model with mean around 3.17, the chance of 4+ protests in a month is about 0.39. I shade the near-term forecast slightly downward because the trailing-year average was probably boosted by the late-2024 cost-of-living unrest in Martinique, so the current underlying rate may be a bit lower than that annual average. Offsetting that, the final baseline could slip below 3 as protest-heavy late-2024 days roll out of the reference window, which would make 3 protests sufficient and raise the YES chance. Netting those effects gives a probability a bit below 40% for the first date, then slightly closer to 0.5 for later dates as uncertainty increases and future mobilization could revert upward. I keep the farthest date only modestly higher because the Dec-Jan window may be somewhat holiday-suppressed.

A date one week later (Nov 2) is only modestly cooler in expectation than Oct 26, so weather variability still leaves a meaningful chance it ends up warmer. By Nov 25, the seasonal cooling is much larger, so the chance of beating Oct 26 is materially lower, though not negligible because daily temperatures are noisy and Oct 26 itself could be anomalously cool or warm. I am not using any information after 2025-10-26.

Reference class: same-station comparisons of daily mean temperature between a late-October day and later autumn/winter days in inland central France. Using a climatological outside view, Clermont-Ferrand should cool meaningfully from late October into late November and January. A late-October daily mean is plausibly around ~10–11°C, early November only a little cooler on average, late November several degrees cooler, and late January much colder. Because daily means have substantial day-to-day weather variance, the colder dates still have some chance to exceed 2025-10-26, but the chance declines with seasonal cooling. My rough estimates are therefore about 40% for 2025-11-02, 22% for 2025-11-25, and 13% for 2026-01-24. The biggest unknown is the actual realized temperature on 2025-10-26: if it was anomalously cold, all three probabilities would be higher; if anomalously warm, lower.

Reference class: same-station, same-season comparisons of daily mean temperature across calendar dates. My outside-view anchor is Bordeaux’s climatological annual cycle: late October is typically milder than early November, much milder than late November, and far milder than late January. A rough normal-model approximation is: Oct 26 mean around 14–15°C, Nov 2 around 13–14°C, Nov 25 around 9–10°C, and Jan 24 around 7–8°C, with day-to-day/interannual noise of roughly 3–3.5°C. Comparing two uncertain daily values at those spreads gives something like a ~40% chance for Nov 2 to beat Oct 26, ~15–20% for Nov 25, and high-single-digits for Jan 24. I round to 0.41, 0.17, and 0.09. Although forecast uncertainty grows with horizon, the seasonal cooling from late October to late November and especially late January is strong enough to dominate that effect.

Reference class: pairwise comparisons of daily mean temperature at one Mediterranean station across calendar dates. My outside-view anchor is the seasonal temperature cycle at Marignane plus day-to-day weather variability. Late October is typically warmer than early November by a little, and warmer than late November / late January by a lot. Using rough climatological means for the area, Oct 26 is likely around the mid-to-upper teens C, Nov 2 only ~1-2 C cooler on average, Nov 25 roughly ~5-7 C cooler, and Jan 24 colder still. Daily mean temperatures do vary by several degrees, so there is still meaningful overlap for Nov 2 and a smaller tail for the later dates. I therefore put the chances at about 38% for Nov 2, 14% for Nov 25, and 17% for Jan 24; the Jan estimate is kept only slightly above the late-Nov estimate because the longer horizon increases uncertainty and leaves more room for unusual warm-spell / cool-Oct-26 combinations, even though the seasonal mean is lower.

Reference class: pairwise comparisons of daily mean temperature at a tropical maritime Southern Hemisphere station between late October and later spring/summer dates. Base rate: later dates in austral spring are somewhat more likely than not to be warmer than late October, but the annual cycle at small oceanic islands is usually modest, so single-day weather noise keeps probabilities fairly close to 50%. Juan de Nova should have a mild warming climatological drift from Oct 26 into early/late November, with a likely peak or near-peak by austral summer; however, marine moderation, cloud/rain in wet season, and possible cyclone effects prevent high confidence. So I put Nov 2 modestly above even, Nov 25 a bit higher due stronger seasonal shift, and Jan 24 still above even but pulled back toward 0.5 because the horizon is much longer and wet-season variability is larger.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in the Moody’s Baa minus 10-year Treasury spread. Base rate is close to 50% for any future date being above the current level. I adjust slightly upward because the latest known value before the forecast date (1.710 on 2025-10-16) is a bit below the spread’s longer-run typical level, so mild mean reversion toward a wider spread is somewhat more likely than not. Offsetting that, the exact 2025-10-26 comparison value is unknown and could differ from 1.710, and short-run spread moves are noisy and heavily affected by Treasury yield swings as well as credit risk sentiment. So I end with only a modest >50% lean for all three dates, with farther horizons pulled closer to 0.5.

Reference class: short-horizon moves in high-grade US corporate bond index yields. Outside view, the chance that a yield series is above its current level after 1 week to 3 months is usually close to 50%, because Treasury yields and credit spreads are volatile and hard to predict over these horizons. I make only a slight upward adjustment for the near dates: AA corporate yields can rise modestly on small increases in Treasury term premium or mild spread widening, and 4.430% is not obviously an extreme level that strongly implies mean reversion. But the exact 2025-10-26 reference value is unknown, and over the longer horizon there is more room for offsetting forces such as Fed easing expectations, growth slowdown, or spread tightening, so the forecast moves back toward even odds.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in US investment-grade corporate effective yields. Outside view, these moves are close to a coin flip, because yields are market prices and near-term changes are dominated by macro/news noise. I add only a slight upward lean because the last known BBB effective yield was 4.930 on 2025-10-16, which looks roughly moderate-to-slightly-low versus the higher-yield environment since 2022, leaving some room for mild mean reversion upward and/or wider credit spreads. Offsetting that, late-2025 policy and growth uncertainty could just as easily pull Treasury and corporate yields lower, and the exact 2025-10-26 comparison value is not known here. So I stay very close to 50%, with the longer-horizon probabilities pulled closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in the seasonally adjusted 4-week moving average of continued claims. Outside view is roughly 50/50 for being higher vs lower at a future date, but this series is smoothed and therefore somewhat persistent. Given the latest known value before the comparison date was 1.93M as of 2025-10-16, I lean modestly toward continued elevation in the very near term. I keep the edge small because the exact 2025-10-26 comparison value is itself unknown and may already include some late-October movement, and because longer horizons allow more mean reversion or macro re-acceleration in either direction. Hence: slightly above even odds for 2025-11-02, still above even odds but closer to 50/50 for 2025-11-25, and only a small lean above 50/50 by 2026-01-24.

Reference class: short-horizon direction-of-change forecasts for large, seasonally adjusted bank loan aggregates, especially CRE loans. Outside view, these stocks are sticky and often drift upward nominally, so the base rate for being above a recent level is modestly above 50%. But CRE is a pressured segment, banks have faced tighter lending conditions, and the exact 2025-10-26 baseline is unknown. That leads me to a small YES edge rather than a strong one. I give the nearest date the highest probability because aggregate loan levels usually move gradually; for longer horizons I pull probabilities back toward 0.5 to reflect rising uncertainty about macro conditions, CRE stress, and the unseen baseline value.

Initial belief state: Reference class = weekly/monthly comparisons of the Treasury General Account in normal cash-management periods, starting from a roughly mid-range balance (~$794B per the prompt). Base rate is close to 50/50 because Treasury actively manages the TGA around a target band, but calendar effects matter. Key uncertainties are the exact 2025-10-26 baseline value, any debt-ceiling or unusual financing constraints, and the precise timing of auction settlements, tax receipts, and large outlays. My initial/final view: slightly above even odds for the first date because it is effectively only about one weekly observation later and month-end financing/persistence can keep the balance elevated; below even odds by late November because a late-October level may already reflect mid-October inflows and November often sees some cash drawdown before mid-December taxes; then back to just above even odds by late January because mid-December and mid-January tax receipts can rebuild cash, though the longer horizon adds uncertainty and pulls the estimate closer to 0.5.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in very short-dated Treasury yields, especially 6-month constant-maturity rates that mostly reflect expected Fed policy over the next several meetings. Outside view says the sign of a 1-week to 3-month move is usually close to 50/50 because market rates incorporate known information quickly. I shade below 50 because a 6-month yield around 3.8% in late October 2025 is more consistent with a stable-to-mildly easing policy path than with a renewed hiking cycle, and the Nov/Dec FOMC window creates somewhat more downside than upside risk. The effect is modest because any expected easing is likely already priced. So: near coin-flip by Nov 2, somewhat lower by Nov 25, then back a bit closer to 50 by Jan 24 as forecast uncertainty rises.

Reference class: short-horizon moves in the weekly, seasonally adjusted FRED series for total deposits at all US commercial banks. Outside view, nominal bank deposits usually have a mild upward drift over time, but at weekly-to-quarterly horizons the series is noisy and can be pressured by money-market competition, Treasury cash-flow effects, and policy/liquidity changes. I therefore put the odds just above even for all three dates, with the edge fading toward 0.5 as horizon increases and uncertainty compounds.

Reference class: short-horizon changes in front-end Treasury bill yields, especially 4-week bills around FOMC windows. Outside view is close to 50/50 for a later value being above today’s value, but with a mild tilt downward here because the latest known 4-week bill rate (4.020 on 2025-10-16) looks more consistent with a Fed that is on hold or easing slightly than with renewed hikes. For 2025-11-02, I put the chance below even because the 4-week tenor moves closer to the early-November FOMC meeting; if there is any cut probability priced, that creates a small mechanical downward bias. By 2025-11-25, there is still a mild downward tilt, but more room for repricing in either direction. By 2026-01-24, two-sided macro uncertainty is larger, so the forecast moves back toward 50%. Main uncertainties are the exact 2025-10-26 reference value, how FRED handles weekend dates, and the market-implied odds of November/December Fed cuts as of the cutoff.

Reference class: short-horizon changes in the Eurosystem/ECB weekly total-assets balance sheet during a quantitative-tightening regime. Outside view: since the 2022 peak, the balance sheet has been structurally shrinking as APP/PEPP holdings run off, so a future reading being above a late-October 2025 baseline is less likely than not. For the 1-week horizon, though, weekly statement noise is large enough that an increase is still fairly plausible. By late November, the ongoing runoff makes an increase materially less likely. By late January, the structural downtrend still points down, but year-end/turn-of-year balance-sheet fluctuations and the longer horizon add uncertainty, so I move that probability somewhat back toward 0.5 relative to the 1-month horizon.

Reference class: short-horizon moves in the US average weekly retail diesel price relative to its current level. Outside view, these are close to a coin flip: over 1- to 13-week horizons, retail fuel prices are noisy and heavily driven by crude/distillate swings that are hard to predict. I make only a small upward adjustment because late fall can modestly support diesel via distillate/heating demand and freight activity, but I keep that edge small because (a) the exact 2025-10-26 baseline is unpublished/unknown, and (b) horizon uncertainty should pull longer-dated estimates back toward 0.5. So I end up slightly above even odds for early November, barely above for late November, and essentially even by late January.

Reference class: future observations of a mean-reverting implied commodity volatility index relative to a current reference level, over roughly 1-week, 1-month, and 3-month horizons. Base rate is near 50% for 'future value higher than current value,' but I shade slightly below 50 because the latest known OVX reading before the cutoff (37.9 on 2025-10-16) looks somewhat elevated relative to calmer regimes, and implied vol indices tend to mean revert after elevated readings. For the more distant date I pull the forecast closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty, per the horizon instruction.
