I’d put this at 32%. My outside-view anchor is that since 2022 the US has repeatedly revised AI/advanced-computing export controls roughly annually as loopholes and strategic concerns emerged, which would normally support a fairly high chance of another move. But by the 2025-10-26 forecast date, the relevant window is already mostly over, and broad EAR/BIS rule changes are harder than targeted enforcement actions. The January 2025 package was already sweeping, so any additional qualifying measure would likely need to be either a replacement broad rule, a meaningful expansion on cloud-compute access, or a modification of model-weight controls. Those are plausible, but not more likely than not to be implemented in the final two months if they have not already clearly appeared. So I land below 50%, but still materially above a low base-rate because this policy area is unusually active and national-security driven.

I estimate an 11% chance. My outside-view anchor is that Congress very rarely enacts a new, substantive federal regulatory regime for an emerging technology quickly, and the remaining window from 2025-10-26 to 2026-01-01 is very short. The prompt itself indicates that, even late in the period, the notable enacted/advancing AI bills are things like TAKE IT DOWN and CREATE AI, which it says would not count, while broader AI regulatory frameworks still have not been established. That suggests the field has high salience but low legislative conversion into qualifying law. A YES outcome is still possible through a narrow bipartisan AI-specific provision attached to a year-end package, but absent evidence of a near-final conference-ready bill with qualifying mandates, the default should be low. I therefore put the probability a bit above a pure base-rate floor, but still clearly below even odds.

My estimate is 10%. As of 2025-10-26, the coalition had planning momentum and publicly discussed a reassurance force, but actual deployment still appeared contingent on a ceasefire or peace arrangement that had not materialized. With only about two months left in 2025, the event requires both a political trigger and a rapid operational decision. Because Russia strongly opposes such a force, several European states are cautious, and reporting had suggested movement away from direct troop deployment toward alternative security arrangements, the most likely outcome is no coalition-designated military presence in Ukraine by year-end. I leave some probability for a small initial deployment, advance element, air mission, or maritime mission if diplomacy shifts suddenly.

On the plain-text resolution criteria, this looks very likely to be YES already by the cutoff: European states had previously announced transfers of M270/MARS-II type MLRS to Ukraine, and those are explicitly listed as qualifying HIMARS-compatible systems. That would make the event effectively already satisfied before 31 Dec 2025. I discount from near-certainty because the question is oddly worded for a prospective market, so resolvers might implicitly intend only new 2025 commitments; if so, the remaining ~2 months after the cutoff create some uncertainty.

I estimate a low chance. The threshold of 15 bcm in a single quarter is very high relative to the likely 2025 EU import capacity from Russia after the shutdown of Ukrainian transit. By 2025, remaining Russian gas to the EU is mainly via TurkStream plus LNG. Even allowing for winter seasonality and the EU's April 2025 decision to postpone LNG restrictions, those channels look unlikely to reach 15 bcm in any one quarter without an unexpected reopening or expansion of pipeline routes. Since only one quarter needs to exceed the threshold, I leave a small tail probability for an LNG spike or routing surprise, but the most likely outcome is that all four quarters stay below 15 bcm.

Outside view: the world’s richest person at a year-end is usually one of a very small set of people, and since 2021 Musk has been in that top cluster more often than anyone else, but not dominantly so. Inside view: Musk has unusually large upside from multiple assets (Tesla, SpaceX, and potentially other private-company valuations) that could keep him at #1, but his wealth is also more volatile than rivals’ and the question resolves on a single day, which increases the chance that a market move leaves someone else marginally ahead. Without late-2025 Bloomberg standings or other fresh evidence, I’d make him a modest favorite but clearly below 50/50-plus-confidence territory.

Estimated from the 2025-10-26 vantage point, the chance is low but nontrivial. Outside view: Iran has threatened Hormuz closure many times, and has harassed shipping, but a true market-resolving shutdown/severe restriction is rare because it is highly escalatory and self-damaging. Inside view: 2025 featured unusually high direct Iran–Israel/U.S. tensions, and the market’s YES threshold is broader than a total blockade, so the risk is higher than the historical base rate. But the strongest evidence against YES is that even after the year’s peak escalation, Iran still had not actually closed the strait by late October. With only about two months remaining, I put the remaining probability around 11%.

Initial belief state: Reference class = formal declarations of war between long-running rival states, especially nuclear-armed rivals. Base rate is extremely low: modern interstate conflicts, including India-Pakistan crises, usually occur without formal declarations. Case-specific adjustment: 2025 did see a serious India-Pakistan military crisis, which raises risk versus an ordinary year, but by 2025-10-26 the year's peak escalation had already passed without either side issuing a formal declaration, and only about two months remained. Because the resolution criterion is very narrow, I put this near the floor at 5%.

I estimate a 9% chance. My reference class is founder-CEOs of very large public companies stepping down over a near-term horizon. The outside view says CEO turnover is not rare over a full year, but it is meaningfully lower for founder-led, highly founder-influenced firms, and much lower over only the final ~2 months absent an already-signaled succession. Musk-specific factors cut both ways: he is unusually distracted across multiple ventures and has elevated governance/legal/operational risk, which raises the chance of a sudden change; but he also has strong board influence, a large equity stake, a long history of remaining in place despite controversy, and Tesla is tightly identified with him. Forced removal seems hard; death/incapacity is possible but very low. Because I do not have search tools here to verify late-2025 developments, I lean on structural priors and keep confidence low.

My estimate is 28%. Outside view: true public driverless ride-hailing launches are rare and Tesla in particular has a long history of missing aggressive autonomy timelines. Inside view: by the 2025-10-26 cutoff Tesla appeared materially closer than in prior years, with a limited Austin robotaxi pilot and very permissive Texas regulation, so a qualifying launch by year-end was still plausible. But this market’s standard is stricter than Tesla’s PR standard: no in-car observer, no human backup, bona fide non-tester rides, 10,000 cumulative rides, and service active on Dec. 31. With only about two months left after the cutoff, the biggest hurdle was not the 10k ride count once open, but removing the in-car monitor/observer and keeping the service running without a safety or PR setback. That keeps me well below 50%.

Reference class: short-horizon rebounds in national fertility rates after a record low in an ultra-low-fertility developed country. Outside view alone, I’d start below 50% because South Korea’s decline had been persistent for years. But conditioning on information available by 2025-10-26, the balance tilts clearly toward YES: pre-cutoff official reporting indicated 2024 had already risen above 2023’s 0.72, and 2025 births/marriages were still running higher year-over-year. That means 2025 would have to give back the 2024 rebound and then some to finish at 0.72 or lower. Possible, but not the likeliest outcome.

As of 2025-10-26, Musk was still Tesla CEO, so this is effectively a ~2-month hazard-rate forecast. For entrenched founder-like CEOs of major public companies, the chance of departing over just two months is usually very low. I bump above that low base rate because 2025 brought unusual pressure on Musk/Tesla: weak operating performance, governance criticism, and credible public reporting that the board had at least explored succession planning earlier in the year. But the strongest forces still point to continuity through year-end: Tesla’s board has historically been highly aligned with Musk, he publicly indicated he expected to remain CEO for years, and by late October there was no confirmed successor or formal transition plan. Net: a real but still small chance of a late-2025 exit.

Base rate is low because the US and Iran have avoided an openly acknowledged full-spectrum war for decades despite many crises. But the prompt’s June 2025 updates imply that direct US-Iran kinetic action had already occurred in mid-2025, which raises the conditional risk above the usual background level. Still, the creator set a fairly high bar: isolated strikes or a brief exchange are not enough; it needs to look like sustained war in public and media terms. With only about two months left as of 2025-10-26, my estimate is that a YES resolution is possible but clearly more likely than not to fail.

Reference class: MLS teams, especially expansion teams, qualifying for the MLS Cup Playoffs. A generic MLS team has about a 60% chance to make the playoffs under the current format, while recent expansion teams are closer to roughly 50-55% in their inaugural seasons. I’d start San Diego FC around that range, then adjust upward because my pre-cutoff recollection is that San Diego had a notably strong inaugural campaign and was around or above the Western Conference playoff line by late 2025; given the cutoff date is after the regular season or essentially at its end, that would make qualification quite likely. I’m stopping short of near-certainty because I can’t verify the exact standings or clinch status without search tools, and expansion teams can fade late. Net: high but not definitive probability.

Reference class: near-term forecasts of a flagship crewed lunar landing mission actually putting humans on the surface. Base rate is extremely low when, at the forecast date, no mission is in final launch posture and major precursor milestones remain unfinished. As of 2025-10-26, the only plausible path to YES was NASA’s Artemis III, but that program had already slipped beyond 2025 and still depended on multiple critical prerequisites (Artemis II, lunar lander readiness, suits, integration). No other national or private program appeared remotely close to landing humans on the Moon before year-end 2025. So YES would require an extraordinary schedule pull-in or surprise mission. I therefore put this at the floor of my allowed range.

Reference class: a highly volatile mega-cap stock touching a round-number downside barrier within a calendar year. Base rate anchor: TSLA historically has frequent 30%+ to 50%+ drawdowns, so a sub-$200 print in a given year is far from rare. However, conditioning on the forecast date being late October 2025 matters a lot: if TSLA has not already traded below $200 by then, only about two months remain, which materially lowers the remaining probability. My biggest uncertainty is the exact 2025 price path through 2025-10-26—especially whether TSLA had already dipped below $200 earlier in the year. Balancing Tesla’s very high volatility and nontrivial chance of an earlier breach against the shortened remaining window, I estimate a 38% chance the market resolves YES.

Reference class: near-term forecasts of U.S. headline CPI crossing a specific threshold late in a disinflation cycle. My base rate is roughly 50%: when inflation is around the upper-2s/low-3s and policy is still restrictive, getting below 2.5% within about a year is plausible but far from automatic. I then adjust down modestly because conditioning on a Trump presidency raises the chance of tariff-, immigration-, and fiscal-related price pressure in 2025, even if the full effects may take time to pass through. With only about six weeks between the knowledge cutoff and resolution, the key issue is whether headline CPI is already very near the threshold; absent direct late-2025 BLS data here, I think slightly-below-even is the best calibrated estimate.

Because the Nobel component had already effectively resolved NO by the forecast date, this market is equivalent to: 'Will there NOT be a bilateral Ukraine ceasefire before 2026?' As of 2025-10-26, there were only about two months left in the year, the Russia-Ukraine war was still active, and the parties’ war aims remained far apart. In that reference class—major interstate wars still actively fought late in the year without an agreed truce—an end-of-year bilateral ceasefire is uncommon. I allow some chance of a limited or politically driven late-year truce, but the short timeline and entrenched conflict make NO on the ceasefire more likely than not, so YES on this market is high-probability.

I estimate a 9% chance. Outside view: a specific non-political tech brand getting its own SNL sketch in a given year is uncommon. Waymo is more culturally salient than a typical tech company because robotaxis are novel and meme-able, which lifts the chance somewhat, but SNL is still more likely to parody 'self-driving cars' generically or Tesla/Elon specifically than to do a brand-specific Waymo sketch. Given the late-October forecast date, there is also limited runway left in the 2025 SNL calendar if it has not already happened.

I’d put this at 34%. The strongest pro-YES factor is that Musk apparently went beyond idle speculation and publicly signaled intent to create a party, and this market’s bar is relatively low: it does not require ballot access or even actual candidates, only a tangible, media-recognized party-like entity. But the background also suggests the situation remained ambiguous months after the initial announcement—enough that the creator repeatedly clarified that a tweet alone is not sufficient and ultimately leaned on a subjective ‘I know it when I see it’ standard. That pattern is more consistent with a headline-grabbing declaration than a clearly operational party. Musk has the money and platform to make this happen quickly, so YES is very live, but absent clearer evidence of infrastructure by the Oct. 26 cutoff, NO remains more likely.

Reference class: abrupt loss of control by an entrenched authoritarian regime. The outside view is that such regimes rarely collapse in any given year, and the probability is even smaller once they have already survived most of the year. Conditional on information up to 2025-10-26, Iran had not already undergone regime change, so only about two months remained for a coup, revolution, or state collapse to remove the regime from Tehran. Iran does face real tail risks—elite fracture, succession turmoil, regional-war spillover, or mass unrest—but the regime has historically shown strong coercive capacity, elite-security cohesion, and resilience through severe protests and leadership shocks. So YES is possible but still unlikely by 2025 year-end.

Initial reference class: highly publicized, technically deep reliability/safety problems in general-purpose AI systems being declared effectively 'solved' on a near-term deadline. Base rate for such questions is low: these problems are usually mitigated substantially, but rarely disappear across broad real-world use, especially when the resolution standard is 'the outstanding problem no longer exists.' As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, my outside-view and inside-view both point strongly to NO: frontier LLMs had improved a lot through better post-training, retrieval/tool use, and domain-constrained workflows, but hallucination was still a standard caveat in deployment, evaluation, and product use. In open-ended generation, models could still produce confident falsehoods; in high-stakes use cases, mitigation layers were still needed rather than the problem being considered fixed. Because the forecast date is only about two months before resolution, there is very little time for a step-change large enough to make the broader 'outstanding hallucination problem' disappear by end-2025. The main reason not to go all the way to the floor is that the market is somewhat subjective: an evaluator could interpret 'fixed' as 'reduced enough in common products/workflows that it is no longer a major practical issue.' But under the stated criterion, the bar is high, and I think the problem still very likely existed at year-end 2025.

Initial belief state: Reference class = active civil lawsuits involving public-facing internet personalities/content creators, likely defamation/harassment/media-related, that are still unresolved near the end of the forecast year. Base rate: most civil cases settle eventually, but conditioning on a case still being active this late materially lowers the chance of settlement within the remaining ~2 months; for late-stage, high-conflict public disputes, a rough outside-view rate is around 30-45% for settlement by a near-term deadline. Key uncertainties: whether a trial date was set before year-end, whether mediation/settlement talks were active, whether there were dispositive motions likely to force leverage, and whether the parties were pursuing vindication rather than cost minimization. Given no search tool is available here, I cannot update on case-specific facts, so I set an initial/final estimate slightly below even odds.

This looks extremely unlikely. The outside view is that a very large lower-middle-income country starting around 40% adult account ownership does not usually reach 95% within seven years. Nigeria would have needed a roughly 55-point jump from the 2017 World Bank level in the background. Digital finance and agent banking could accelerate growth, and the resolving definition may be broader than traditional bank accounts, but 95% adult account ownership is still an exceptionally high bar. My estimate is therefore at the floor of the allowed range.

Initial outside-view forecast: the legal bar here is high ("treaty obligation," not just a declaration), the timeline to 2026 is very short, and I am not aware of any mature intergovernmental negotiation track that was likely to produce a 10-country binding restriction on solar geoengineering by end-2025. I assign a small residual chance for (a) an existing treaty forum adopting language that resolvers judge sufficient, or (b) a small coalition of states moving unusually fast on a limited non-use pact.

Base rate is extremely low: there are essentially no prior cases of purported extraterrestrial artifacts from ambiguous recovered debris being confirmed in peer-reviewed literature as alien technology, let alone on a schedule this short. The resolution standard is also exceptionally strict: a systematic review of material from the IM1 site must be peer-reviewed, use at least 3 independent labs, and conclude with 95% confidence both non-terrestrial origin and technological origin/no known natural process. Given the controversy over whether IM1 was even interstellar, the difficulty of proving the recovered spherules came from that object rather than terrestrial contamination, and the much harder step of ruling out all natural explanations for composition, the chance of clearing that bar before 2026 appears very small. I assign the floor-allowed 5% rather than lower only because Loeb had already recovered and was analyzing material, so there remains some small possibility of a late paper making a strong claim.

Reference class: concentrated, expensive mega-cap tech leadership cohorts over a 1-2 year horizon. Those cohorts do sometimes crack badly, but this specific bar is very high: at least four of seven must each close 50% below their adjusted all-time highs. My ex-ante anchor would have been around the low-20s because the setup clearly had bubble characteristics. But conditioning on the forecast date of 2025-10-26, only a bit more than two months remain until resolution. Unless several names were already quite close to the 50%-down line by late October, resolving YES would probably require a very fast, broad tech crash comparable to the sharpest historical episodes. That is possible, especially with AI-cycle and macro risks, but still unlikely over such a short remaining window. So I land at 7%.

My best estimate is 68%. On a strict like-for-like CDC attributable-deaths basis, 70,000 by end-2025 looks too high: the public CDC benchmark moved from roughly 23k to roughly 35-37k over several years, so a clean doubling by 2025 is not the most likely path. But the question allows 'another credible source,' and by late 2024-2025 there were already broader AMR burden studies and summaries that plausibly put US annual deaths linked to resistant infections above 70k, especially under 'associated with' rather than narrowly 'attributable to' definitions. With only about two months left from the cutoff, the main issue is whether an existing or imminent credible report clearly qualifies, not whether the underlying epidemiology suddenly doubles. That pushes me above even odds, but not near certainty because resolution may hinge on whether broader AMR mortality estimates are judged to count as deaths 'from' antibiotic-resistant infections.

Reference class: major AAA video games with announced release windows, especially Rockstar tentpole releases with global distribution. Base rate: if such a game remains on schedule for the target year, Europe availability is usually very likely; but once a flagship game is officially delayed into the following year, the chance it still goes on sale in Europe during the original year becomes extremely low. Here, Europe itself is not the bottleneck—Rockstar routinely sells GTA titles broadly across Europe—so the question is essentially whether GTA VI would actually launch in 2025. As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the key fact is that Rockstar had already moved GTA VI to May 2026 earlier in 2025. With only about two months left in the calendar year and an official 2026 date already set, a 2025 European on-sale date would require a highly implausible reversal. I therefore put this near the floor at 5%.

Estimación final: 69%. Mi ancla base es ligeramente alcista: Bitcoin ha terminado años naturales en positivo una mayoría modesta de veces, y los años pos-halving han sido especialmente fuertes. Además, 2024 fue muy alcista y el entorno político de EE. UU. en 2025 era más favorable a cripto. Todo eso empuja la probabilidad por encima de 50%. Aun así, con solo unos dos meses por delante desde el 26 de octubre de 2025, la variable crítica es el nivel exacto del precio en esa fecha; y Bitcoin puede caer 10-20% en ese plazo con bastante facilidad. Por eso no me voy a una probabilidad muy alta. En conjunto, el sesgo alcista estructural supera el riesgo de corrección, pero no por un margen enorme.

I estimate about an 8% chance. By late October, the Atlantic still can produce hurricanes, especially in the Caribbean, but storms exceeding 160 mph are exceptionally rare this late in the season. The strongest argument for YES is that 2025 has already produced Erin at 160 mph, which implies a season/environment capable of extreme intensity and makes a second top-end storm more plausible than in an average year. Still, the remaining seasonal window is short and the historical frequency of >160 mph Atlantic hurricanes after late October is very low, so NO remains the clear favorite.

Chegg appears genuinely distressed: its core product has been structurally damaged by generative AI, revenue is falling, it has announced major layoffs, and the background notes shrinking cash and very large losses. Those facts make bankruptcy a live possibility. But the key issue is timing: the forecast window from the 2025-10-26 cutoff to 2026-01-01 is only about two months. Even badly stressed public companies often try additional cost cuts, debt exchanges, asset sales, or refinancing talks before filing, unless there is an immediate liquidity crisis or debt maturity/default inside that window. Based only on the information provided here, I see meaningful risk, but not a more-likely-than-not chance, of a filing before year-end. High-single-digits feels most defensible.

I put this at 7%. Outside view: most 2–4σ particle-physics anomalies do not mature into peer-reviewed 5σ discovery claims on a fixed schedule. Inside view: the original 2021 R_K lepton-universality anomaly later moved much closer to the Standard Model, and the remaining LFU tensions I know of (notably R(D), R(D*)) were still only around the ~3σ range rather than on the brink of 5σ. The deadline is also unusually restrictive: as of 2025-10-26, only about two months remain, and the question requires a peer-reviewed paper published by 2025-12-31, not just a preprint or conference hint. I leave a small tail for a rapid combination/summary paper or an already-in-press result crossing 5σ, but overall NO is much more likely.

My best estimate as of 2025-10-26 is that Mikie Sherrill is a modest but clear favorite. The outside view is that New Jersey is Democratic-leaning, but gubernatorial races there are much more competitive than presidential results and Republicans have often won the governorship. Sherrill looks like a strong statewide Democratic nominee, and a Trump presidency would generally help Democrats in a blue-state off-year race. Still, this is an open seat after two Democratic terms, and a credible Republican like Jack Ciattarelli keeps the race very live. Net: Sherrill favored, but far from certain.

This looks very unlikely. The relevant reference class is annual U.S. customs-duty revenue totals. Historically these have been on the order of tens of billions of dollars, with the question background itself pointing to roughly $82.2b as the recent baseline. To land between $500b and $1t in FY2025, the U.S. would have needed an extraordinary step-change to more than 6x that level within a single fiscal year. Even allowing for the major 2025 tariff increases, FY2025 only included part of the year under the new tariff regime, and actual collections would have had to average over $41.7b per month across the full fiscal year to clear $500b. That is far above the recent historical run rate and would require a very extreme effective tariff take on a huge import base with limited exemptions and limited import compression. Because FY2025 had already ended by the knowledge cutoff, the remaining uncertainty is mostly about final Treasury accounting/publication rather than the underlying economic reality. My true estimate would be lower, but given the instruction floor on probabilities, I submit 5%.

Base-rate view: Anthropic is one of a small handful of labs with a real chance to own the top Chatbot Arena model by end-2025, but it is not the clear favorite versus OpenAI and Google. I’d put Anthropic in the second-tier-to-cofavorite range: strong enough to lead, but still somewhat less likely than the field combined and a bit behind the largest rivals. Arena-style human preference evaluations are a relatively good fit for Claude’s strengths, and Anthropic’s alphabetical tiebreak is slightly favorable against many likely rivals, but OpenAI/Google’s compute, product scale, and release cadence keep Anthropic below 50%. With no web-search tool available, this forecast relies on pre-cutoff general knowledge and outside-view reasoning, so confidence is low.

Base rate and structural factors point against Kaiser. In Chile, the first-round leader is usually a major coalition candidate with broad organization and national appeal. Kaiser’s plausible path depends on a fragmented field and a right-populist surge large enough to let him overtake more established right-wing rivals. That is possible, but still a relatively narrow route, so I put his chance at about 16%.

Reference class: an NBA Eastern Conference team winning its conference in a given season. The naive base rate is about 1 in 15 teams (~6.7%), but for a team entering the season from a non-contender/retooling position, the rate is materially lower. The Nets, as of this forecasting date, look much more like a long-shot than an average East team: conference winners almost always have elite top-end talent, strong recent regular-season performance, and roster continuity. Brooklyn’s realistic path to YES would require a much stronger-than-expected young-core leap, a major star acquisition, or a chaotic/injury-hit Eastern field. Those paths exist, but they are unlikely. Given the forced minimum range, I put them at the floor-like end of the allowed probabilities.

I estimate a low probability that Moonshot owns the top Chatbot Arena model on 2025-10-31. The outside view is that leadership on major public LLM leaderboards is usually concentrated among a small set of frontier labs with the most compute, talent, and distribution advantages. Moonshot is a credible AI startup, but compared with the most likely competitors for #1—labs like OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Meta, and possibly other well-capitalized challengers—it would need to outperform a very strong field at exactly the snapshot time. I leave some meaningful probability because fast-moving model races can produce surprises and Arena rankings can shift with a single strong release, but absent specific late-2025 evidence, Moonshot looks like an underdog rather than a favorite.

Initial belief state: Reference class is extraordinary constitutional mechanisms for involuntarily displacing a sitting US president, especially Section 4 of the 25th Amendment. Base rate is effectively near zero: Section 4 has never been used, no president has ever been removed this way, and even impeachment has never produced a Senate conviction/removal. As of the 2025-10-26 forecast date, only about two months remain until resolution, which compresses the already tiny hazard further. For a YES, there would likely need to be a sudden and obvious incapacity event plus willingness by the vice president and a majority of the cabinet to act, and probably overwhelming congressional support if Trump contested. Given Trump’s tendency to appoint loyalists, the political threshold is exceptionally high. My unclipped estimate would be below 5%, but I am using 5% as the floor.

My estimate is 5%. As of 2025-10-26, the strongest outside-view fact is that a Russian territorial offensive against Finland would be an attack on a NATO member, a much rarer and more escalatory class of event than Russia’s prior invasions of non-NATO neighbors. The inside-view also points down: no invasion had occurred in the first five months of the market window, Russia was still heavily committed elsewhere, Finland is highly prepared militarily, and only about two months remained. The main path to YES is not a large conventional invasion but a limited, confirmed grey-zone or border-seizure operation during a broader Russia-NATO crisis. My unconstrained belief would be very low; I submit 5% as a cautious floor estimate.

Initial belief state: reference class is late-horizon forecasts on whether US headline CPI (12-month change) will breach a fixed threshold within the remaining months of a calendar year. Base rate is modest once inflation is already below the threshold and only a few monthly releases remain; historically, moving from roughly the 2-3% area to 4.1%+ within one quarter requires a meaningful shock or strong base effects. My key uncertainties are: (1) how close CPI already was to 4% in the latest available 2025 report by the 2025-10-26 cutoff, (2) whether tariffs or other price shocks were passing through quickly, (3) whether energy prices were spiking into year-end, and (4) how favorable/unfavorable late-2024 base effects were. I set the probability at 8% because by late October only the Oct/Nov/Dec 2025 CPI releases can still trigger YES if it has not already happened, and crossing 4.1% from a likely sub-4 starting point would require a fairly sharp and rapid inflation reacceleration. That is plausible, but still a clear tail outcome rather than the base case.

Reference class: a specific NFL team winning the Super Bowl, evaluated around late October of the season. The raw base rate for any team is about 1/32 = 3.1%, but the Chiefs are not an average team: with the Mahomes/Reid era, they belong in the small class of perennial top-tier contenders that often carry roughly 10-20% title chances in-season. Balancing that against NFL parity, injury risk, and the single-elimination playoff format, I put Kansas City at 16% to win Super Bowl LX as of 2025-10-26. This is above a generic contender but well below even-money because one team must survive several high-variance playoff games.

Outside view first: this is a single-elimination heads-up poker title, likely with a 32- to 64-player invited field. In that reference class, an average entrant’s title odds are only about 1.5% to 3%. Matt Salsberg is a real, accomplished poker player rather than a pure celebrity entrant, so his true chance should be somewhat above a random seat, but absent specific bracket/field/results information, he still does not project as anything close to a likely winner. Given the sparse information available here, I keep him near the low end at 5%.

Initial reference class: individual would-be Best Picture contenders, especially big-budget franchise sequels. Base rate is low: only one film wins, and AMPAS rarely gives Best Picture to VFX-heavy franchise epics/sequels. Avatar has some upside because James Cameron films can become major technical/cultural events and prior Avatar films reached the Best Picture lineup, so nomination is plausible. But winning is a much higher bar: the Academy has historically been more willing to nominate this kind of film than to award it the top prize, and without post-release reviews/precursors there is too much uncertainty to justify a higher number. Net: plausible nominee, but still a clear long shot to actually win.

Reference class: Spotify’s annual global most-streamed artist. Historically this title is captured by a very small set of artists with overwhelming current-year streaming momentum, usually driven by massive recent releases and very strong younger/global streaming audiences. Eminem is an all-time huge artist, but as a legacy act he is not the typical profile of the Wrapped #1 artist, and he has never been the annual global Spotify leader. He would likely need an exceptional 2025 run—multiple huge hits or a dominant release cycle—to beat more natural contenders like Taylor Swift, Bad Bunny, Drake, The Weeknd, etc. That is possible, but still unlikely. I put it at 6%.

Base rates dominate here. A formal NATO Article 5 invocation is extraordinarily rare—historically only once—and NATO has shown a strong pattern of avoiding formal invocation even during serious crises, preferring deterrence, consultations, and proportional responses short of collective-defense activation. With only the final ~2 months of 2025 remaining at the forecast date, the event would likely require a clear, attributable, major armed attack or similarly extraordinary incident against a member state. That is possible in a high-tension environment, but still unlikely. My true outside-view estimate is in the low single digits; I round to 5% as the closest allowed forecast.

Reference class: annual global Spotify 'most streamed artist' winners. That title is very concentrated among a few megastars and tends to be sticky year to year; Billie Eilish has had enormous song-level success but has not historically been the annual overall #1 artist. As of 2025-10-26, most of the counting window is already over, so this is largely a question of whether she was already near the top year-to-date. Her chances are helped by strong carryover from her 2024 album cycle and touring, but overall I still favor the field—especially incumbent-scale streamers with larger catalogs and broader always-on global consumption. So I put Billie at a meaningful but clearly minority chance.

Reference class: formal public self-declarations of AGI by a frontier AI lab. The raw historical base rate is essentially zero, but OpenAI is the single most plausible candidate and the market resolves on an announcement, not on broad scientific consensus. Even so, by the 2025-10-26 cutoff there has been no such announcement yet, only about two months remain, and OpenAI has meaningful strategic reasons to avoid explicitly using the AGI label. My best estimate is therefore a low but non-trivial 11% chance of an OpenAI or official-representative AGI announcement before 2025-12-31 ET.

Initial outside view: Monad is in the reference class of heavily funded, pre-token crypto infrastructure projects (especially L1s/L2s with active testnets and strong community airdrop speculation). In that class, an eventual token launch is common, and a real community airdrop is fairly common, but the exact timing is highly uncertain and often slips. My base-rate anchor is roughly one-third for 'token + genuine airdrop within the next ~month' when a project is late-stage but has not yet clearly executed. That keeps YES very live, because Monad has strong incentives to launch a token eventually and reward early users. But the bar here is strict: it needs a transferable token and an actual airdrop by Nov. 30, not just points, locked rewards, or NFT distribution. Large token launches usually telegraph themselves with foundation setup, tokenomics, exchange coordination, eligibility tooling, or a firm launch date; absent confidence on those as of the cutoff, a 35-day window is short. Delays are common even for top projects. So I land below even odds but meaningfully above zero.

Reference class: novel zoonotic coronaviruses progressing to a WHO-declared pandemic within the same year. The outside view is overwhelmingly negative: many animal coronaviruses are identified, but only a tiny fraction ever achieve sustained human transmission, and an even smaller fraction lead to an explicit WHO pandemic declaration. The February 2025 bat-coronavirus report is concerning but, by itself, is much weaker evidence than confirmed efficient human-to-human spread. With the forecast date already at 2025-10-26, only about two months remain until resolution; absent strong contrary evidence before that date, the chance that WHO newly declares a distinct coronavirus pandemic by year-end is very low. I therefore set this near the minimum allowed probability.

Reference class: October values in NASA GISTEMP under the current warming regime. Historically, most Octobers have been below 1.10°C, but the recent climate trend and the exceptionally warm 2023–2024 period make that old base rate too low. A reasonable outside-view anchor is that October 2025 is likely near the threshold: long-term warming pushes the expected October anomaly to roughly the low 1.1s, while a post–El Niño cooling / neutral-to-weak-La-Niña state by late 2025 gives some chance of dipping under. With no live October 2025 daily-data tool access here, I treat <1.10°C as slightly less likely than not.

Base rate for a 4-team NFL division is 25%, and even incumbent division winners usually repeat well under 50%. But the Chiefs are a special case: with Patrick Mahomes/Andy Reid and a long AFC West winning streak through the 2024 season, they deserve to be the favorite entering 2025. Against that, the AFC West has become more competitive, and without access here to the actual 2025-10-26 standings, injuries, and tiebreaker position, I should not push the estimate too high. Net: Kansas City is a modest favorite, not a lock.

Reference class: exact three-meeting FOMC rate-path sequences during a likely easing phase. Unconditionally, any exact 3-meeting pattern is uncommon, so the base rate for cut-pause-cut is only around the low teens. But this specific sequence is more plausible than average in a gradual, non-crisis easing regime because September and December are natural meetings for policy recalibration, while October is an intervening meeting where a pause is relatively more likely. The main drag is that YES requires all three legs to line up exactly, including the already-occurred September meeting being a cut and December still delivering another cut rather than a second pause or a consecutive October cut. With no direct current-data verification available here, I’d put it below even odds but materially live.

Reference class: frontier AI labs competing for the top spot on Chatbot Arena at a fixed future date. Base rate: among the handful of serious frontier contenders, a non-incumbent but credible lab like Moonshot has a low-but-real chance to jump to #1 over a two-month horizon, roughly high single digits. My estimate stays low because the top Arena position has usually been concentrated among larger labs with stronger recent track records and bigger release cadence; for Moonshot to win by Dec. 31 it likely needs a clearly standout late-2025 launch plus rapid Arena uptake. I still leave meaningful probability because Arena leadership can change quickly after a strong release, and Chinese labs have shown they can surprise the field.

Reference class: a specific monthly NASA GISTEMP anomaly landing in a narrow 0.05°C bracket. Outside view, if the bracket is near the expected monthly mean, such bins often carry roughly 20–35% probability. My inside-view estimate is that October 2025 was likely to be cooler than the 2023/24 El Niño peak but still elevated by the underlying warming trend, putting the most likely October 2025 anomaly somewhere around the low-to-mid 1.1s °C relative to 1951–1980. That makes 1.10–1.14°C a live outcome, but probably a bit less likely than the broader set of outcomes slightly above it. With no web-search tools available, I’m relying on historical GISTEMP patterns, trend, and ENSO reversion logic, so confidence is only medium-low.

I’d put this at 26%. The outside-view anchor is that an exact 25 bp cut at any given scheduled FOMC meeting is not the modal outcome; no-change is much more common, and larger moves happen in sharper downturns. I adjust upward because, from the restrictive 2024 starting point, a late-2025 easing/normalization path was plausible. I adjust back down because this is one specific meeting: by October 2025 the Fed could already have done much of its cutting and paused, or, in a recessionary scenario, could prefer 50 bp or have moved earlier. Net: a meaningful but clearly sub-50 chance of an exact 25 bp cut.

Initial outside-view forecast only, with low confidence because no search/web tools are available to check the late-October-2025 ballot and polling. Reference class: relatively low-profile state legislators/insurgent ideological candidates trying to finish top-two in a NYC mayoral general election. The base rate is low because NYC mayoral generals are usually dominated by the Democratic nominee, with second place often going to a better-known Republican or independent. For Mamdani to finish second, he would need to become a major citywide contender yet still not win outright—possible, especially if the field fragments, but uncommon. So I keep the estimate near the low end, slightly above the floor to reflect his real progressive profile and the possibility of an unusually chaotic race.

Reference class: lower-budget, non-elite Ligue 1 clubs trying to win the title in a league that has been heavily dominated by PSG, with only occasional wins by a small set of richer challengers. Le Havre fit the profile of a club whose title odds are ordinarily extremely low. Without any evidence available here of a dramatic October 2025 title challenge, ownership transformation, or collapse by the usual powers, the outside view points strongly to NO. My unconstrained estimate would be lower than 5%, but under the task's floor I submit 5%.

My best estimate is 10%. The relevant reference class is a named non-incumbent challenger in a major-city mayoral race, likely against an incumbent. In that class, incumbents usually win and any single challenger is typically a clear underdog unless late polling, fundraising, or endorsements show otherwise. I do not have strong candidate-specific late-October evidence in hand pointing to Reeves as the favorite, so I keep him as a live but fairly unlikely winner.

Reference class: a specific frontier AI lab holding the #1 spot on Chatbot Arena at a future checkpoint. Outside view, by late 2025 there are roughly 4-5 credible contenders for the top text model, but the slot has historically been concentrated among OpenAI/Google/Anthropic, so xAI’s base-rate share is below an even split. I anchor around 15-20%, then adjust slightly upward because xAI had become a serious frontier lab with major compute and a plausible path to a top-tier Grok release by late 2025. I keep it below that of OpenAI/Google because the leaderboard is highly competitive, can change quickly after new releases, and xAI also loses exact ties on the market’s alphabetical tiebreak rule. Net: xAI is a real contender, but still not the modal outcome.

Initial reference class: exact 3-meeting FOMC outcome paths late in a policy cycle. Base-rate-wise, one specific sequence like cut-pause-pause is uncommon; before conditioning on late-2025 data I’d anchor in the low-teens. By the 2025-10-26 forecast date, the September meeting outcome is already known and is decisive: if September was not a cut, this path is effectively dead; if it was a cut, October pause is fairly plausible but December pause is still far from certain. Lacking direct verification tools here, I lean toward a low probability overall because the path is narrow and many plausible states of the world imply either no September cut or an additional December cut.

I’m at the floor because the outside-view probability of a direct US–Venezuela military engagement in a roughly 10-week window is extremely low. Even allowing for a broad definition that includes coast-guard/naval exchanges or damaging ramming, actual kinetic contact between US and Venezuelan uniformed forces has been very rare historically. By the 2025-10-26 forecast date, only a few days remain in the resolution window, so unless a qualifying incident had already happened earlier in the window, the remaining hazard is very small. The main unresolved factor is whether anything had already occurred between Aug. 21 and Oct. 26, but absent verified reporting, the best base-rate-driven estimate remains low.

Base-rate/outside-view forecast only. The reference class is late-horizon forecasts of a U.S. territorial invasion/occupation of a sovereign state. In the post-Cold War era, such events are extremely rare, and an offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory within the remaining 2025 window would likely require a conspicuous crisis and military buildup. While U.S.-Venezuela relations can be hostile and regional escalation is not impossible, the specific resolution standard is much narrower than sanctions, covert action, naval incidents, or limited strikes. Given the very low base rate and the short remaining time window, I put this at the floor 5%.

I estimate an 8% chance. The key consideration is the very short remaining window: as of the 2025-10-26 forecast date, only five days remain until resolution. Conditional on an Israel-Iran direct-fire ceasefire having already survived for months after the June 2025 war, the hazard of a renewed qualifying strike in any given five-day span is relatively low. That said, the underlying conflict remains volatile, nuclear-related triggers could still spark a sudden escalation, and either side retains both motive and capability to strike quickly. On balance, the short horizon dominates, so I land in the high-single-digits rather than the teens or higher.

Initial belief state: Reference class = U.S. interstate invasions/territorial seizures, especially against large hostile regional powers. Base rate is extremely low in any given ~2 month window, and even lower when the target is a large, populous state like Iran. The main upside case is that a broader U.S.-Iran crisis in 2025 could have escalated into a limited seizure of an island, port, or border area, which would satisfy the market even without a full Iraq-style invasion. But the resolution criteria are strict: airstrikes, missile attacks, naval clashes, or covert action would not count unless the U.S. intended to establish control over territory. By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, there was no known U.S. territorial offensive in Iran already underway, leaving only about two months for a major escalation. Occupying even a small part of Iran would be a very high-cost, high-escalation move that U.S. policymakers generally avoid in favor of stand-off strikes and deterrence. So the probability is very low; constrained by the platform rule not to go below 5%, I submit 5%.

Reference class: a specific global superstar being Spotify’s most-streamed artist in a given calendar year. The outside view is not generous: recent winners have been concentrated in just a couple of acts (notably Bad Bunny and Taylor Swift), and The Weeknd—despite enormous catalog strength—has not been the year-end winner in that recent run. That suggests a base rate in the low teens for him specifically, even though he is clearly in the small top tier of plausible contenders.

I then adjust upward because The Weeknd has unusually durable global streaming power: his catalog is one of the strongest on Spotify, and a major album cycle in 2025 would increase his chances meaningfully because it adds fresh streams on top of a very high baseline. Still, annual top-artist status depends more on total yearly consumption than on snapshot monthly listeners, and he has historically been stronger on the latter than on actually finishing #1 for the year. The field also likely remains very competitive, especially from artists like Bad Bunny and Taylor Swift.

Net: he is a serious contender, but not the most likely single artist. I put him at 18%.

Base rate for a previously unregulated/offshore prediction venue to launch a compliant US real-money market on a fixed near-term deadline is low to moderate, since CFTC-related approvals, ownership transfer, compliance, and product rollout often slip. I still put this slightly above even because Polymarket had unusually strong brand momentum, user demand, and incentive to re-enter the US, and the most plausible path would be operating through an already-licensed DCM rather than building from scratch. The main reason this is not much higher is the resolution standard: there must be an actual public real-money trade on a regulated, Polymarket-operated DCM by year-end, and with only about two months left as of the forecast date, execution/timing risk remains substantial.

Base rate is extremely low: since 1979, regular US ground combat troops entering Iran has effectively not happened, and even during severe crises the US has strongly preferred sanctions, air/naval strikes, covert action, and partner operations over putting regular troops on Iranian soil. The market’s threshold is also unusually high—special operations forces, advisers, contractors, and mere overflight do not count—so YES likely requires a major overt escalation. With only about two months left after the 2025-10-26 cutoff, I put the chance at 6%: not impossible if there were a dramatic spiral or retaliatory raid, but still clearly unlikely.

'A Minecraft Movie' already posted an unusually high opening (about $162.8M domestic 3-day) and, as of 2025-10-26, no 2025 release had topped it. From that date, the main remaining threats were 'Avatar: Fire and Ash,' 'Zootopia 2,' and 'Wicked: For Good.' My outside view is that a late-October opening-weekend leader often survives to year-end, but not always when a true mega-sequel remains. The biggest risk is Avatar: 'The Way of Water' opened around $134M, so Avatar 3 would need a meaningful step up to clear Minecraft's mark. That's plausible but not more likely than not. 'Zootopia 2' and 'Wicked: For Good' are also credible challengers, but each would likely need a major overperformance to reach the mid-$160Ms on a 3-day basis. Net: YES is somewhat favored, but far from certain because one or two late-year event films still had real upside.

Reference class: which frontier lab holds the #1 spot on Chatbot Arena’s text leaderboard at a fixed date. Outside view, with roughly four major credible contenders by late 2025 (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, plus a smaller ‘field’), a specific lab starts around the low-to-mid 20s percent. I move Google a bit up from that base rate because Gemini models were highly competitive through 2025 and Google has both the capability and incentive to ship another strong update before year-end. I keep it below one-third because the competition is intense, the horizon is only about two months from the cutoff, and OpenAI/Anthropic/xAI all have realistic paths to leading the Arena by Dec. 31. Google also gets a small tie-break benefit versus several rivals, though not versus Anthropic. Net: Google is a serious contender, but not the single dominant favorite.

Initial/reference-class view: explicit WHO pandemic declarations are very rare events. In recent decades, the clear formal examples are essentially 2009 H1N1 and 2020 COVID-19, implying a low annual base rate. Conditioning further on the fact that by the 2025-10-26 cutoff there had not been a globally salient WHO pandemic declaration, only about two months remained in the resolution window. The main upside risks were ongoing zoonotic and cross-border outbreak threats (especially influenza/H5N1-type scenarios and mpox), but WHO generally uses the word 'pandemic' sparingly and often prefers emergency language instead. So the residual chance of a declaration before 2025-12-31 looks low but not negligible.

As of 2025-10-26, the key question is effectively whether Starmer will resign, be removed, or announce departure in the final ~2 months of 2025. The outside view is strongly against this: UK prime ministers who have just won a large parliamentary majority rarely leave office within roughly their first 18 months, and the remaining time window is short. The main upside risk to a YES would be severe political collapse, scandal, health issues, or a sudden internal Labour move, but absent clear evidence of an imminent challenge I would keep the forecast in the high-single-digits rather than anywhere near even. I therefore estimate a 7% chance of YES.

Reference class: an individual entrant winning a televised single-elimination heads-up poker championship. Outside view, a player's chance is roughly 1/N adjusted for skill; for likely field sizes in this kind of event, that usually lands in the low single digits. I have no strong pre-cutoff evidence that John Smith was a top favorite, already deep in the bracket, or otherwise had a major edge by 2025-10-26. Given the high variance of heads-up poker, a non-favorite still has some upset equity, but without specific positive information he looks like a long shot. I therefore put him at 5%.

Reference class: which lab owns the #1 Chatbot Arena model at a near-term checkpoint. Outside view says the overall top spot is usually concentrated among a few frontier labs, and a secondary contender like Z.ai would normally be a low-single-digit to ~10% possibility. With only five days to resolution, probability mass should sit mostly on whoever is already leading or has an obviously imminent launch. My best estimate is that Z.ai is unlikely to overtake the field in that short window, and the market’s alphabetical tiebreak is unfavorable to Z.ai, so it likely needs a clear outright lead rather than a tie. I therefore assign 5%.

Reference class: year-over-year declines in the BEA/FRED federal current expenditures series large enough to exceed $250b SAAR between adjacent Q4s. Outside emergency unwinds or major one-off accounting/subsidy changes, that kind of nominal drop is uncommon because Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, veterans benefits, and interest usually keep rising. DOGE/Elon created real pressure for cuts, but this particular resolution metric is hard to move quickly: much of it is mandatory spending, Congress controls a lot of the rest, and personnel/contract cuts often phase in slowly or save less than headlines imply. Also, a $250b drop in the SAAR level still requires a meaningful net decline after offsetting entitlement and interest growth. By late Oct. 2025 there was limited time left for Q4 to produce such a large net year-over-year fall unless there were sizable one-off BEA accounting effects or unusually aggressive/implemented rescissions. So YES is possible, but still clearly the minority case.

I’m assigning a low probability because a 17-week consecutive run at #1 on the Billboard 200 is an extreme outcome in the modern chart environment. Even very large superstar albums usually fail to hold the top spot uninterrupted for that long, because new major releases, front-loaded consumption, and seasonal competition tend to break streaks. Without reliable up-to-cutoff evidence here about the album already being deep into such a run, the outside view dominates. I am interpreting the question as effectively asking whether the album reaches at least 17 straight weeks at #1; if it instead means exactly 17, the true probability would be even lower.

Outside-view, this is a 'which frontier lab holds the #1 spot on a fast-moving public leaderboard at a specific future date?' question. DeepSeek is a real contender, but not the modal one: year-end Chatbot Arena leadership is more likely to be held by one of the larger, heavily productized frontier labs such as Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, or xAI. DeepSeek’s probability is still meaningfully above a random-lab baseline because it has shown an ability to make sudden capability jumps and a late-2025 release could plausibly contend for #1. The short horizon helps if DeepSeek is already close, but hurts because a single-date resolution means it must not only reach the top but still be there after likely competitor releases. The alphabetical tiebreak is mildly favorable versus several likely rivals, but not enough to outweigh the broader competitive disadvantage. Net: plausible, but still a clear underdog.

Base rate first: for Bitcoin, a 10–20% retracement or even a brief exchange-specific wick below a nearby round-number threshold is common over a multi-month window. The resolution rule is also YES-friendly because it only needs one Binance 1-minute candle low at or below $99,999.99, not a sustained move or a close. With roughly two months remaining after the 2025-10-26 cutoff, that makes a sub-$100k print somewhat more likely than not, assuming BTC is trading in the low six figures rather than far above the threshold. My biggest uncertainty is that I cannot inspect contemporaneous Jul–Oct 2025 price history here; if BTC had already dipped below $100k earlier in the window, the true probability would effectively be near 1, while if BTC were already far above $120k–$130k and had held that level for months, the remaining probability would be materially lower. Given those uncertainties, I land modestly above 50% with low confidence.

I’d put Elon Musk at about an 8% chance. The outside-view base rate for any specific person winning TIME’s Person of the Year in a given year is low, and repeat winners are uncommon. Musk’s probability is meaningfully above a generic famous person because he is one of the few figures with plausible cross-domain influence in politics, AI, social media, autos, and space, and TIME has already shown willingness to select him. But strong competition from presidents, war leaders, broader movements, or a thematic/collective choice keeps his odds in the single digits.

Outside-view forecast: a 16-straight-week run at #1 on the Billboard 200 is extraordinarily rare, and this market also requires the album to debut at #1 and be released on time. Without verified, cutoff-compliant chart-run evidence showing this album was already deep into such a streak by 2025-10-26, the base rate dominates. I therefore put this near the floor at 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, the key fact is that 2025 already has a very high fixed benchmark: A Minecraft Movie opened around $163M domestically, which Zootopia 2 would have to beat. That is a tough target for a Thanksgiving Wednesday-launched animated sequel when resolution uses only the Friday-Sunday 3-day figure, a format that tends to understate holiday family films versus standard Friday launches. Zootopia 2 is still a serious contender because the first film was a billion-dollar hit and Disney animated sequels can open very large, but a more plausible 3-day range looks roughly in the $120M-$150M area, with upside into the $160Ms only if it becomes a true event. Even if it clears Minecraft, Avatar 3 still remains a later, credible challenger. Balancing those factors, I put Zootopia 2's chance to finish as 2025's top domestic opener at about 17%.

With no search tool available, I have to rely on base rates rather than event-specific reporting. For high-profile political shootings/assassination attempts with an identified suspect, the modal outcome is that the attacker is found to be a lone actor; true co-conspirator cases are materially less common. I discount that prior somewhat because this market resolves NO not only if accomplices are found, but also if Tyler Robinson turns out not to be the shooter or the case is dropped. Netting those together, I land at roughly a 72% chance of YES as of 2025-10-26.

Reference class: which frontier lab owns the #1 Chatbot Arena model at a specified future date. A naive outside view with ~4 serious contenders gives roughly 25%, but OpenAI deserves a premium because it has been a persistent frontier leader, has exceptional compute/talent, and is highly incentivized to optimize for chat-preference performance. Against that, the field is very competitive, Arena rankings can shift quickly with new releases, and OpenAI is hurt by alphabetical tiebreaks against labs like Anthropic and Google. With only about a two-month horizon from the cutoff to resolution, OpenAI looks like a clear contender but not a majority favorite.

Lean No. By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, most of October had already elapsed. So this resolves YES only if BTC/USDT had already printed a 1-minute low at or below $90k earlier in the month, or if it suffered a sharp late-month liquidation wick in the final five days. Bitcoin is volatile and Binance 1-minute lows can overshoot, so the chance is not trivial, but absent evidence of an earlier touch, a drop to $90k that late in the month is materially less likely than not.

My forecast is 5%. Outside-view, lower-profile minor-party candidates in competitive two-round presidential systems almost never go on to win, and Chilean presidents in the democratic era have generally come from major national coalitions or clear front-runners. Francesca Muñoz has at least some theoretical path in a fragmented field because a runoff can produce surprises, but absent strong evidence that she had become a top-two contender by 2025-10-26, her chances look very small. I would ordinarily place this below 5%, but I am respecting the instruction to keep probabilities within the allowed range.

Initial outside-view forecast: direct interstate kinetic engagements between U.S. forces and Venezuelan forces over a matter of days are extremely rare absent an ongoing war or acute crisis. As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the event window has only a few days left, which sharply limits opportunity for a qualifying incident. There are some nonzero tail risks from Caribbean maritime/air encounters, counternarcotics interdiction, or spillover from the Essequibo/Guyana dispute, but a reportable exchange of force between official U.S. and Venezuelan military units would be a major escalation and is still unlikely. If unconstrained I would probably be a bit below 5%, but following the instruction floor I submit 5%.

Valencia are a historic club, but the modern La Liga title base rate for anyone outside Real Madrid, Barcelona, and Atlético is extremely low. Valencia have not been at title-winning level for many years, and absent strong current-season evidence to the contrary, the outside view dominates. Given the lack of search tools here, my estimate leans heavily on long-run league structure and Valencia’s recent competitive level. This is a floor-level YES probability.

Using an outside-view estimate only: for announced albums notable enough to attract a market, a minority actually debut at #1 on the Billboard 200, and this contract also counts a delay/non-release by year-end as 0 weeks. Without reliable artist-specific release-week data available here, the safest estimate is that '0 straight weeks at #1' is more likely than not, but not overwhelming. I put it at 70%, with low confidence because the most important specifics are unknown.

My estimate is 17%. Outside view: only a tiny set of global superstars ever finish as Spotify’s year-end most-streamed artist, and Drake is one of the few with the catalog size and historical precedent to do it. But by late October 2025, most of the Wrapped counting period is effectively already baked in, and Drake still faces a very high bar against recent dominant winners and other massive 2025 streaming competitors. My best read, using information available up to 2025-10-26, is that Drake is a plausible contender but not the likeliest leader.

Base rate first: allied nuclear breakout into an independently controlled, operational arsenal on a sub-1-year timeline is extraordinarily rare. Among NATO members and Major Non-NATO Allies, no non-nuclear ally has newly crossed that threshold in decades, and doing so in 2025 would require either a clandestine near-finished program or an unprecedentedly rapid overt political and technical breakout. As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, there were prominent discussions in some allied states about nuclear options and deterrence, but no broadly credible confirmation that any eligible US ally had already obtained an independently controlled nuclear weapon. With only about two months left until resolution, the remaining path to YES looks very narrow. The main upside scenario is a state with substantial latent capability and strong security incentives making a sudden breakout or revelation; South Korea is the clearest example, with Japan a more remote latent-capability case. But even for them, the political, legal, technical, and diplomatic barriers to fielding an operational weapon under full national control by Dec. 31, 2025 remain huge. So this should be very low, but per the required bounds I put it at the floor.

My estimate is 8%. The outside view is that a genuine definitive cause of autism will not be identified on a politician’s timetable, especially within a few months, so the scientific base rate is extremely low. I adjust upward because this market resolves on RFK Jr.’s public announcement rather than scientific consensus, and he had reason to make a bold claim after publicly setting expectations in 2025. But as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, only five days remained, and a qualifying announcement would likely have been a major news event; absent clear evidence that it had already happened, YES looks unlikely but not impossible.

Peru winning the 2026 World Cup is an extreme long shot. My unconstrained estimate is well below 1%, but I am clipping to the minimum allowed 5%. The outside view is overwhelming: World Cup titles are won by a tiny set of elite nations, and Peru are not in that class. As of the 2026 cycle they were also a weak side in CONMEBOL qualifying, so qualification itself was doubtful/already likely failed by late Oct 2025. Even if they did qualify, their tournament win equity would still be tiny.

Initial belief state: Reference class = a calendar year immediately following a record-hot year in the modern warming era, especially when the previous record was likely boosted by El Niño. Base rate for 'the very next year also becomes the new hottest year' feels around 15-25%, but I adjust downward because 2024 likely set an unusually high benchmark. Key uncertainties that would most change this estimate are the exact NASA GISTEMP Jan-Sep/Jan-Oct 2025 anomaly versus 2024, the expected Nov-Dec ENSO/ocean-state contribution, and the exact size of 2024's lead. My final estimate is 12%: ongoing long-run warming gives 2025 a real chance, but a post-El Niño follow-on year usually cools somewhat relative to the peak year, so absent strong evidence that 2025 was already ahead by late October, the path to a new annual record is fairly narrow.

Reference class: ownership of the #1 Chatbot Arena model on an arbitrary late-2025 checkpoint among frontier AI labs. My outside view is that the top slot is most likely to be held by one of a small set of labs—OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and xAI, with others as long shots. xAI is a serious contender by late 2025 because of its funding, compute ambitions, and ability to tune for chat preference, but it started later than the leading incumbents and historically the very top conversational models have more often come from those incumbents. The market’s tiebreak rule also hurts xAI, since it would lose most ties on alphabetical order. Initial belief state: Reference class is short-horizon crypto price-barrier questions — whether a highly volatile large-cap token prints a specified intramonth high within the remaining days of the month. Base rate: for major crypto assets, a 5-day barrier hit is quite sensitive to how far spot is below the target; if the target is ~10-20% above spot, chances can be in the 15-30% range during bullish conditions, but if it requires a >30% jump they fall to low single digits. With only a few days left in October and $350 being a very ambitious level for SOL, I anchor in the mid-teens. Key uncertainties are the exact SOL price and momentum as of 2025-10-26, whether October had already come close to $350, and broader crypto market strength. My best estimate is 17%: not negligible because SOL can make violent late-cycle moves, but more likely than not it stays below $350 before month-end.

Reference class: an incumbent long-course swimming world record over very short horizons (1 week, 1 month, 3 months), especially after the main long-course championship season. Outside view: swimming world records are rare events, and a record that has already survived for many years usually has a low hazard of falling over the next few months. Inside view: Lilly King’s 100m breaststroke record was still the most recent known holder as of 2025-10-16, and late Oct through Jan is not typically the peak period for long-course world-record attempts; many elite swimmers are in short-course season, offseason, or heavy training. That makes persistence highly likely. I shade down a bit for (a) the explicit blind gap between 2025-10-16 and the 2025-10-26 forecast date, so a change could already have happened without us knowing, and (b) the event is competitive enough that an exceptional swim or pending ratification could matter. Hence very high but not near-certain probabilities, declining modestly with horizon.

I’m treating this as a rating-dynamics question for an already established ~2470 classical player. A 1% increase from roughly 2473 is about +25 Elo, which is a large move at this level. With FIDE standard K-factors for established players, +25 usually requires outperforming expectation by roughly 2.5 points across a meaningful number of classical games. Over the first horizon (2025-11-02), that is very unlikely because there is almost no time after the 2025-10-26 baseline, and the Wikipedia page on both 2025-11-02 and 2025-11-25 will very likely still reflect the November list, so the first two probabilities are nearly the same. By 2026-01-24 there is more time for one or two classical events to matter, so the chance rises, but it is still modest because +25 from an already high 2470s base is uncommon and likely implies a new career-high range around 2498+.
